What Is a Notice of Default in Utah? What It Means & What to Do Next
Free Consultation
Education Series · Utah Foreclosure

What is a Notice of Default in Utah?
What it means & what to do next

If you just received one, don't panic. You have more time — and more options — than you think.

Tiffany Lancaster, CDPS-Certified Broker
Tru Agency Team
May 2026
TL;DR — The Short Answer

A Notice of Default is not a foreclosure. It's a warning.

  • It means your lender has officially recorded that you're 90+ days behind on mortgage payments
  • In Utah, your lender must wait a minimum of 120 days after filing before scheduling a foreclosure sale
  • You still have real options — loan modification, short sale, traditional sale, cash offer, or bankruptcy
  • The biggest mistake homeowners make: assuming it's too late and doing nothing
Watch: Tiffany Lancaster explains the Notice of Default in plain English — no legal jargon
Understanding the Basics

You received an NOD — here's what that actually means

If you've received a Notice of Default, you're probably scared. You might be overwhelmed. You may even feel a little ashamed. Tiffany Lancaster hears this from homeowners every single week — and her message is always the same: there are options available to you.

The biggest misconception Tiffany sees? Homeowners confuse a Notice of Default with an actual foreclosure. They're not the same thing. An NOD is the first formal step a mortgage company takes — not the last. It means the clock has started, but the game is far from over.

📋 Utah NOD Key Facts — Know Your Numbers
📅
90+ days behind Minimum before lender can file an NOD
⏱️
120-day minimum Before a foreclosure sale date can be scheduled
📂
Public record Filed with the county — anyone can access it
💰
Fees keep growing Attorney fees and late fees are added after filing
📝
Reinstatement amount The NOD states exactly what you owe to get current
🤝
Communication helps Lenders are less likely to push foreclosure if you're talking
Breaking It Down

What does a Notice of Default actually say?

In plain English, the Notice of Default is a formal document that says: "You are behind by this amount. Here's what you need to pay to reinstate your loan. If you don't take action, we will proceed with foreclosure."

The lender files it with the county — through a trustee (an attorney) — which is why it becomes public record. It includes a reinstatement amount: the exact dollar figure you'd need to pay to get your loan back in good standing and stop the process entirely.

💡

What "reinstatement" means

Paying the reinstatement amount brings your loan fully current and cancels the foreclosure process. It's one of your options — but not your only one. The NOD will state this figure clearly.

The difference between a modification and a partial claim

Tiffany flags this as one of the most misunderstood things she sees. Your bank may offer something called a partial claim — which many homeowners mistake for a loan modification. They're very different things.

A loan modification permanently changes your loan terms (lower rate, extended term, etc.). A partial claim is an additional loan tacked onto the back of your mortgage — another lien against your property that must be paid off when you sell. Servicers don't always make this distinction clear. Know exactly what you're signing.

Your Timeline in Utah

How much time do you actually have?

This is the question Tiffany gets most. The answer: more than most people think — but not unlimited. Here's how the Utah foreclosure timeline works after an NOD is filed:

1
You Are Here

Notice of Default Filed

Lender files with the county after 90+ days of missed payments. The clock officially starts. You receive the notice. Fees begin accumulating immediately.

2
120-Day Window

Mandatory Waiting Period

Utah law requires a minimum of 120 days between the NOD filing and when a foreclosure sale can be scheduled. This is your primary action window. In many cases it's longer — sometimes significantly.

3
If No Action Taken

Foreclosure Sale Date Scheduled

The lender schedules a public auction. More fees are added. However — even now — you still have options right up until the morning of the sale date.

4
Last Resort

Day of Foreclosure Auction

Tiffany has seen homeowners file bankruptcy the morning of the auction to stop the sale. She doesn't recommend waiting this long — but the point is: options exist at every stage. Don't give up.

⚠️

Fees compound the longer you wait

Every step deeper into the process adds attorney fees, late fees, and trustee fees to your balance. Acting sooner means fewer fees on top of what you already owe — and more options available to you.

Your Real Options

What can you actually do right now?

Depending on your situation — how much equity you have, how much time remains, and what your goals are — here are the paths available to most Utah homeowners after receiving an NOD:

🏦

Reinstate the Loan

Pay the past-due amount in full to get current and stop the process entirely. Best if you have access to funds or family support.

📋

Loan Modification

Work with your lender to permanently change your loan terms — rate, payment, or length. Takes 30–90 days, requires documentation.

🏡

Traditional Sale

If you have equity, a full MLS listing can get you top dollar. Requires enough time before auction. Tiffany's team handles this.

⚡

Cash Offer

Sell fast to a trusted cash buyer. Closes in 7–14 days. Best when auction is approaching or speed is the priority.

🤝

Short Sale

If you owe more than the home is worth, a short sale lets you sell with lender approval — far better for your credit than foreclosure.

⚖️

Bankruptcy Protection

Filing triggers an automatic stay that immediately halts foreclosure. Chapter 13 lets you catch up over 3–5 years. Has long-term consequences — consult an attorney.

✅

Short sale vs. foreclosure — the credit difference is massive

Tiffany describes foreclosure as "an atomic bomb to your credit." A short sale, by contrast, only reports your last late mortgage payment — meaning you could potentially qualify to buy a home again in as little as one year from your last late payment, assuming your credit is otherwise in good shape.

What to Do Right Now

The two most important calls you can make

Tiffany's advice comes down to two concrete actions — and the order matters:

1

Call your mortgage company

Let them know you're aware of the situation and actively working on a plan. Lenders are far less likely to push hard toward a sale date when they know you're communicating and engaged. Silence is the worst thing you can do.

2

Call Tru Agency Team — free consultation

Sit down with one of our CDPS-certified agents. Explain your situation. We'll walk through every option that applies to your specific circumstances — even the ones that don't pay us a commission. No pressure. No judgment. Just clarity. (435) 244-3210

⚠️ Watch Out for Foreclosure Rescue Scams

Because your NOD is public record, your mailbox is about to fill up. Not everyone reaching out has your best interests in mind.

  • Anyone pressuring you to sign something you don't fully understand
  • Offers requiring you to transfer your title to them "temporarily"
  • People telling you to stop communicating with your lender
  • Claims of "special relationships" with banks or government programs
  • Upfront payment requests before any work is completed
  • Anyone who says this is your "only option" and won't explain alternatives
Tiffany's rule: "Who are the people willing to give you free advice whether it benefits them or not? Trust those people."
Frequently Asked Questions

Everything homeowners ask Tiffany about NODs

No. A Notice of Default is the first formal step in a process that could lead to foreclosure — but only if no action is taken. In Utah, the lender must wait a minimum of 120 days after filing the NOD before they can schedule an actual foreclosure sale. You have time and real options at this stage.
Utah law requires a minimum of 120 days between the NOD filing and the earliest possible foreclosure sale date. In practice, many cases take significantly longer — lenders aren't required to move as fast as the minimum allows. That said, don't count on extra time. Take action as soon as possible so your options stay open.
Because Notices of Default are public record, filed with the county. Anyone who accesses county records can see them — and many investors, attorneys, and companies actively track these filings to reach out to homeowners. Some are legitimate helpers. Others run predatory "foreclosure rescue" schemes. Be very careful what you sign and who you trust.
The reinstatement amount is the total you'd need to pay to bring your loan fully current — this includes all missed payments, late fees, and attorney fees accrued since the NOD was filed. If you can pay this amount, the foreclosure process stops entirely and your loan is restored. Your NOD document will state this figure. Note: this amount grows the longer you wait.
A loan modification permanently changes your loan terms — potentially lowering your interest rate, extending the term, or restructuring your payments. A partial claim, on the other hand, is an additional loan attached to the back of your mortgage. It's another lien on your property that must be repaid when you sell. Many homeowners don't realize they've received a partial claim rather than a modification — always confirm in writing what exactly you're signing.
Almost always yes — especially for your credit. A foreclosure stays on your credit report for 7 years and severely limits your ability to buy another home. A short sale typically only reports your last late mortgage payment, meaning you could potentially qualify to buy a home again within a year of your last late payment — if your credit is otherwise in order. Tiffany calls foreclosure "an atomic bomb to your credit" compared to a short sale.
Yes. Even after a sale date is set, options remain — including paying off the balance, a last-minute cash sale, lender negotiation, or filing bankruptcy to trigger an automatic stay. Tiffany has personally seen homeowners file bankruptcy the morning of the auction to halt the sale. She doesn't recommend waiting that long, but the point is: never assume it's too late without talking to someone first.
Nothing. The consultation and options review are completely free. Tru Agency only earns a commission if you choose to move forward with a short sale or cash offer — and only if that's genuinely the right option for you. Tiffany's team will tell you about options that don't pay them (loan modifications, bankruptcy, government assistance) if those options are a better fit for your situation.

Key Takeaways

  • 1
    A Notice of Default is not a foreclosure — it's the first formal step in a process that requires at least 120 more days before a sale can even be scheduled in Utah.
  • 2
    The biggest mistake is doing nothing. Fees compound, options narrow, and time runs out. Taking action early keeps more doors open.
  • 3
    Your NOD is public record — expect mail. Be very careful who you trust, what you sign, and never stop communicating with your mortgage company.
  • 4
    You have real options: reinstatement, loan modification, short sale, traditional sale, cash offer, or bankruptcy. Which ones apply depends on your equity and timeline.
  • 5
    A short sale is dramatically better for your credit than foreclosure — potentially allowing you to buy a home again within a year vs. a 7-year credit hit from foreclosure.

She holds the Certified Distressed Property Specialist (CDPS) designation and specializes in helping Utah homeowners navigate foreclosure, short sales, and distressed property situations — always putting education before sales.
